splits.legalQuestion: Whether a multiemployer pension plan actuary may select actuarial assumptions for calculating withdrawal liability after the statutory measurement date (the end of the prior plan year), or whether all such assumptions must be fixed by that date.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether ERISA requires actuarial assumptions used to calculate employer withdrawal liability to be locked in by the end of the prior plan year or whether actuaries may adjust assumptions after that date. The outcome affects billions of dollars in potential obligations for thousands of employers participating in multiemployer pension plans.

positions.label: Assumptions must be fixed by measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that all actuarial assumptions must be established by the statutory measurement date (end of the prior plan year), providing employers greater predictability about potential liability.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

positions.label: Assumptions may be selected after measurement date
positions.summary: Holds that plan actuaries may select or adjust actuarial assumptions after the statutory measurement date, allowing use of more current data in calculating withdrawal liability.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.relatedScotusTitle: M & K Employee Solutions v. Trustees of the IAM National Pension Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the term of supervised release is tolled (paused) when a supervisee absconds from supervision, thereby extending the period during which the person can be found to have violated the conditions of supervised release.
splits.description: Circuits split on whether federal courts may apply a judicially-created 'fugitive tolling' doctrine to supervised release, effectively extending the supervision period beyond what the judgment specifies when a person absconds. This affects revocation proceedings for thousands of individuals who abscond from supervised release each year.

positions.label: Tolling applies
positions.summary: Holds that supervised release is tolled during the period a supervisee absconds, extending the court's authority to revoke supervised release beyond the original term.

positions.label: Tolling does not apply
positions.summary: Holds that there is no statutory basis for tolling supervised release when a supervisee absconds, and courts lack authority to create such a doctrine.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2244(b)(1)'s bar on relitigating previously raised claims in habeas proceedings applies to federal prisoners filing motions under Section 2255.
splits.description: Circuits are split 6-to-3 on whether the gatekeeping provision of § 2244(b)(1) — which categorically bars claims previously presented in habeas — applies to federal prisoners bringing successive § 2255 motions. If it applies, federal prisoners face a stricter bar than the requirements of § 2255(h) alone.

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) applies to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1)'s categorical bar on previously raised claims applies to federal prisoners filing successive § 2255 motions, in addition to the requirements of § 2255(h).

positions.label: Section 2244(b)(1) does not apply to § 2255 motions
positions.summary: Holds that § 2244(b)(1) does not apply to federal prisoners' successive § 2255 motions, meaning they need only satisfy § 2255(h)'s requirements of newly discovered evidence or a new rule of constitutional law.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a candidate for federal office has Article III standing to challenge a state law that allows the counting of mail-in ballots received after Election Day.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether candidates have standing to bring pre-election challenges to election administration rules. The question affects whether voting procedure disputes can be litigated before an election or must wait until after, with significant practical implications for election administration nationwide.

positions.label: Candidate has standing
positions.summary: Holds that candidates for federal office have Article III standing to challenge state election laws before an election based on competitive injury from allegedly unlawful ballot-counting rules.

circuits.name: Seventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

positions.label: Candidate lacks standing
positions.summary: Holds that a candidate's alleged competitive injury from state election procedures is too speculative or generalized to confer Article III standing for a pre-election challenge.

circuits.name: Third Circuit
circuits.caseName: Bognet v. Secretary of Commonwealth of Pennsylvania

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Bost v. Illinois State Board of Elections

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the rule of lenity takes precedence over deference to an agency's (or the Sentencing Commission's) interpretation of an ambiguous provision in a criminal or sentencing context.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether lenity trumps Auer/Kisor deference when interpreting ambiguous Sentencing Guidelines or criminal provisions. At least three circuits hold lenity prevails over agency deference, while the Third Circuit held that deference trumps lenity. The issue has grown more significant after Loper Bright overruled Chevron.

positions.label: Lenity trumps deference
positions.summary: Holds that the rule of lenity takes precedence over agency or Sentencing Commission deference when interpreting ambiguous criminal or sentencing provisions, preventing agencies from expanding criminal liability through interpretive guidance.

positions.label: Deference trumps lenity
positions.summary: Holds that agency or Sentencing Commission deference applies even in the criminal sentencing context, and that lenity does not override the Commission's authoritative interpretation of ambiguous Guidelines provisions.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the word 'and' in the First Step Act's safety-valve provision, 18 U.S.C. § 3553(f)(1), is conjunctive (requiring all three criminal-history criteria to disqualify a defendant) or disjunctive (any one criterion disqualifies).
splits.description: Circuits are deeply split on whether 'and' in the safety-valve provision means 'and' or 'or,' which determines whether a defendant must meet all three criminal-history criteria to be disqualified from safety-valve relief or whether possessing any one is sufficient. This directly affects mandatory minimum sentencing for drug offenses.

positions.label: 'And' is conjunctive — all three criteria required to disqualify
positions.summary: Holds that defendants are disqualified from safety-valve relief only if they meet all three criminal-history criteria listed in § 3553(f)(1), reading 'and' as conjunctive.

positions.label: 'And' is disjunctive — any one criterion disqualifies
positions.summary: Holds that possessing any one of the three criminal-history criteria in § 3553(f)(1) is sufficient to disqualify a defendant from safety-valve relief.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Sentencing Guidelines' Application Note 1 to U.S.S.G. § 4B1.2, which includes inchoate offenses (conspiracy, attempt, aiding and abetting) in the definition of 'controlled substance offense,' is entitled to deference when the guideline text itself does not mention inchoate offenses.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether Application Note 1's expansion of 'controlled substance offense' to include inchoate offenses is a valid, authoritative interpretation of the guideline deserving deference under Stinson/Kisor, or whether the guideline text unambiguously excludes such offenses. The split affects career offender designations for defendants with prior conspiracy or attempt convictions.

positions.label: Application Note 1 is entitled to deference
positions.summary: Holds that Application Note 1 is a valid, authoritative interpretation of the ambiguous guideline text and that inchoate offenses qualify as 'controlled substance offenses' for career offender purposes.

positions.label: Application Note 1 exceeds the guideline text
positions.summary: Holds that the guideline text unambiguously excludes inchoate offenses and that Application Note 1 impermissibly expands the definition of 'controlled substance offense' beyond what the text allows.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a prior state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' under the Sentencing Guidelines when the state-controlled substance is not on the federal schedules.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether the career offender guideline's definition of 'controlled substance offense' requires the substance to be controlled under federal law or whether any state-controlled substance suffices. The Eighth Circuit reads the guideline broadly to include any state-law offense, while other circuits require the substance to be federally scheduled.

positions.label: State-controlled substance suffices
positions.summary: Holds that the Guidelines' definition of 'controlled substance offense' encompasses any offense under state law regardless of whether the substance is on the federal schedules, because the text refers to 'an offense under . . . state law' without limiting it to federally controlled substances.

positions.label: Substance must be federally controlled
positions.summary: Holds that a prior state drug conviction qualifies as a 'controlled substance offense' only if the substance involved is also controlled under federal law, grafting a federal-law limitation onto the guideline.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a non-retroactive change in sentencing law (such as the First Step Act's anti-stacking amendment to 18 U.S.C. § 924(c)) can be considered as a factor supporting 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether non-retroactive changes in law, alone or in combination with other factors, may contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release. Some circuits categorically bar consideration of such changes, while others permit courts to consider them. The Sentencing Commission's 2024 amendment to § 1B1.13(b)(6) attempted to resolve this but has faced resistance.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes may be considered
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider non-retroactive changes in sentencing law as one factor, alone or in combination with others, when evaluating whether extraordinary and compelling reasons exist for compassionate release.

positions.label: Non-retroactive changes cannot be considered
positions.summary: Holds that a non-retroactive change in sentencing law, whether alone or combined with other factors, cannot contribute to a finding of extraordinary and compelling reasons for compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A).

splits.legalQuestion: Whether federal prisoners challenging conditions of confinement or BOP decisions affecting the terms (but not necessarily the fact or duration) of imprisonment must proceed via habeas corpus or may bring civil rights or Bivens claims.
splits.description: A longstanding circuit split exists on whether challenges to conditions of confinement (as opposed to the fact or duration of confinement) must be brought as habeas claims or may proceed as civil rights actions. The Supreme Court noted but did not resolve this issue in Preiser v. Rodriguez, and circuits have developed irreconcilable approaches, particularly regarding BOP decisions on matters like First Step Act time credits and prerelease custody.

positions.label: Habeas is exclusive for confinement-related challenges
positions.summary: Holds that challenges to BOP decisions affecting the execution of a sentence, including conditions and terms of confinement, must be brought through habeas corpus and cannot proceed as civil rights actions.

positions.label: Civil rights claims permitted for conditions challenges
positions.summary: Holds that prisoners may bring civil rights or Bivens claims to challenge conditions of confinement even when habeas may also be available, particularly when the claim does not directly challenge the fact or duration of imprisonment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the appeal of an order denying a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees in a criminal case is governed by the civil appeal deadline (60 days for government party) or the criminal appeal deadline (14 days).
splits.description: Circuits split on whether a Hyde Amendment motion is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case (governed by civil appeal deadlines) or part of the criminal case itself. The Seventh Circuit joined the majority view that the civil deadline applies, but a minority of circuits have applied the criminal deadline.

positions.label: Civil deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion for attorney's fees is a civil matter ancillary to the criminal case, and the civil appeal deadline governs the time for filing a notice of appeal.

positions.label: Criminal deadline applies
positions.summary: Holds that a Hyde Amendment motion is part of the criminal case and that the criminal appeal deadline governs.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of a debtor's property belong to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor upon good-faith conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7 under 11 U.S.C. § 348(f)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether post-petition appreciation in property value inures to the bankruptcy estate or to the debtor after conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7. The Ninth Circuit held that appreciation belongs to the estate, while other circuits have held that § 348(f)(1)(A) protects debtors' post-petition equity gains upon good-faith conversion.

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the estate
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion increases in the equity of an asset belong to the bankruptcy estate upon conversion from Chapter 13 to Chapter 7, based on the broad scope of § 541(a).

positions.label: Appreciation belongs to the debtor
positions.summary: Holds that post-petition, pre-conversion appreciation in property value belongs to the debtor, not the estate, under § 348(f)(1)(A), which limits the converted estate to property of the estate 'as of the date of filing of the petition.'

splits.legalQuestion: Whether district courts may expressly impose sanctions at supervised release revocation hearings to punish defendants for their violations, or whether revocation sentences must be limited to the purposes of deterrence and public protection.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether punishment is a permissible purpose of a sentence imposed upon revocation of supervised release. The Sixth Circuit allows district courts to expressly punish defendants for supervised release violations, placing it at the 'extreme' of the split, while other circuits hold that revocation sentences cannot be grounded in punishment.

positions.label: Punishment is a permissible purpose
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may impose sanctions at revocation to punish defendants for their violations of supervised release, not only for deterrence and public protection.

positions.label: Punishment is not a permissible purpose
positions.summary: Holds that revocation sentences must be grounded in deterrence and protection of the public rather than punishment, consistent with 18 U.S.C. § 3583(e)'s exclusion of certain § 3553(a) factors including punishment.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether an order denying a motion to seal documents containing private medical information is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine.
splits.description: Circuits appear divided on whether orders denying motions to seal medical records qualify for immediate interlocutory appeal under the collateral order doctrine. The D.C. Circuit held that medical privacy is a 'high order' value warranting immediate review, while other circuits have applied the collateral order doctrine more restrictively to sealing orders.

positions.label: Immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that an order denying a motion to seal documents referencing private medical treatments and diagnoses is immediately appealable under the collateral order doctrine because medical privacy is a value of 'high order' that cannot be adequately vindicated after final judgment.

positions.label: Not immediately appealable
positions.summary: Holds that orders denying motions to seal generally do not qualify for immediate appeal under the collateral order doctrine, as the privacy interest can be adequately protected through other means or vindicated on appeal from final judgment.

splits.legalQuestion: What legal standards govern claims of discrimination or unfair treatment in the operation of hospitality or business establishments under 42 U.S.C. § 1981 or similar federal law?
splits.description: The Supreme Court granted certiorari to review the Second Circuit's approach to legal standards for discrimination claims in hospitality settings, suggesting a circuit split on the applicable standards for liability and damages in such cases. The decision may resolve disagreements over proof requirements and available remedies.

positions.label: Second Circuit approach
positions.summary: Applied standards that the Supreme Court found sufficiently important or unsettled to warrant review in the context of discrimination claims against hospitality businesses.

positions.label: Other circuits' approach
positions.summary: Applied different standards for evaluating discrimination claims in business establishment contexts, contributing to the circuit split that prompted SCOTUS review.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Aviation Administration Authorization Act of 1994 (FAAAA) preempts state-law negligence claims against freight brokers who arrange transportation of property by motor carriers.
splits.description: The circuits are split on whether FAAAA's broad preemption clause, which prohibits states from enacting laws 'related to a price, route, or service of any motor carrier... with respect to the transportation of property,' extends to preempt state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligently selecting or supervising motor carriers. This affects liability exposure across the trucking industry.

positions.label: FAAAA preempts state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers are 'related to' the broker's services with respect to the transportation of property and are therefore preempted by the FAAAA.

circuits.name: Ninth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Miller v. C.H. Robinson Worldwide, Inc.

positions.label: FAAAA does not preempt state negligence claims against brokers
positions.summary: Holds that state-law negligence claims against freight brokers for negligent hiring or supervision of carriers are not preempted because they do not relate to the broker's 'prices, routes, or services.'

circuits.name: Eleventh Circuit
circuits.caseName: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Montgomery v. Caribe Transport II, LLC

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Federal Insecticide, Fungicide, and Rodenticide Act (FIFRA) preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims based on allegedly inadequate product labeling when the EPA has not required the specific warning.
splits.description: Circuits are split on the scope of FIFRA's preemption of state failure-to-warn claims. The question is whether FIFRA's provision that states cannot impose labeling requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal requirements bars state tort claims that effectively require additional label warnings the EPA has not mandated.

positions.label: FIFRA preempts state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that FIFRA preempts state-law failure-to-warn claims that would require labeling changes beyond what the EPA has mandated, because such claims impose requirements 'in addition to or different from' federal labeling requirements.

positions.label: FIFRA does not preempt state failure-to-warn claims
positions.summary: Holds that state-law failure-to-warn claims survive FIFRA preemption when they are parallel to or consistent with FIFRA's misbranding provisions, because FIFRA does not occupy the entire field of pesticide labeling claims.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Monsanto Company v. John L. Durnell

splits.legalQuestion: Whether a district court may consider factors related to potential errors or unfairness in a defendant's conviction or sentence as part of the 'extraordinary and compelling reasons' analysis for compassionate release under 18 U.S.C. § 3582(c)(1)(A).
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether claims that overlap with habeas corpus grounds — such as alleged errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence — may be considered in compassionate release motions. Some circuits allow such considerations as one factor, while others hold that § 3582(c)(1)(A) cannot be used as a substitute for § 2255 habeas relief.

positions.label: May consider conviction/sentence errors
positions.summary: Holds that district courts may consider claimed legal errors or unfairness in the original conviction or sentence as part of the extraordinary and compelling reasons analysis for compassionate release.

positions.label: Cannot use compassionate release for habeas-type claims
positions.summary: Holds that compassionate release under § 3582(c)(1)(A) is not a vehicle for relitigating the validity of a conviction or sentence, and such claims must be brought under § 2255.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the Religious Land Use and Institutionalized Persons Act (RLUIPA) authorizes individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials.
splits.description: Circuits are split on whether RLUIPA creates a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials in their individual capacities. Some circuits have held that RLUIPA, enacted under the Spending Clause, does not clearly authorize personal-liability damages against individuals who are not direct recipients of federal funds, while others have allowed such claims to proceed.

positions.label: RLUIPA authorizes individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA provides a cause of action for money damages against state prison officials sued in their individual capacities for violations of prisoners' religious exercise rights.

positions.label: RLUIPA does not authorize individual-capacity damages
positions.summary: Holds that RLUIPA does not clearly authorize individual-capacity damages suits against state prison officials because Congress cannot impose personal liability on non-recipient individuals through the Spending Clause.

circuits.name: Fifth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Sossamon v. Lone Star State of Texas

splits.relatedScotusTitle: Landor v. Louisiana Department of Corrections and Public Safety

splits.legalQuestion: Whether the federal law prohibiting unlawful users of controlled substances from possessing firearms, 18 U.S.C. § 922(g)(3), violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users.
splits.description: Following the Supreme Court's decision in Bruen establishing a historical-tradition test for Second Amendment challenges, the circuits have split on whether § 922(g)(3)'s prohibition on firearm possession by users of controlled substances (particularly marijuana) is constitutional. This implicates the intersection of expanding state marijuana legalization with federal firearms law.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is constitutional
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) does not violate the Second Amendment because historical tradition supports disarming individuals whose drug use renders them dangerous or untrustworthy with firearms.

positions.label: Section 922(g)(3) is unconstitutional as applied
positions.summary: Holds that § 922(g)(3) violates the Second Amendment as applied to marijuana users because the government has not demonstrated a sufficient historical tradition of disarming persons for drug use alone.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether workers who distribute baked goods to retail stores qualify as 'transportation workers engaged in foreign or interstate commerce' under Section 1 of the Federal Arbitration Act, thereby exempting them from mandatory arbitration agreements.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on how broadly to read the Section 1 exemption for transportation workers, particularly for last-mile delivery workers who transport goods within a single state as part of an interstate chain of commerce. This affects whether such workers can be compelled to arbitrate employment disputes.

positions.label: Broad reading — last-mile workers exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who are the last link in an interstate chain of commerce, even if operating within a single state, qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce and are exempt from the FAA.

circuits.name: First Circuit
circuits.caseName: Fraga v. Premium Retail Services, Inc.

positions.label: Narrow reading — last-mile workers not exempt
positions.summary: Holds that workers who deliver goods locally within a state do not qualify as transportation workers engaged in interstate commerce merely because the goods previously moved in interstate commerce.

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 47(b) of the Investment Company Act creates a private right of action allowing parties to a contract that violates the ICA to sue in federal court for rescission of that contract.
splits.description: Circuits disagree on whether § 47(b) of the ICA provides an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts made in violation of the Act. This affects the ability of private parties to enforce the ICA's protections against abusive investment company practices through direct federal court actions.

positions.label: Section 47(b) creates a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) creates an implied private right of action for rescission of contracts that violate the Investment Company Act, allowing private parties to sue directly.

circuits.name: Second Circuit
circuits.caseName: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

positions.label: Section 47(b) does not create a private right of action
positions.summary: Holds that § 47(b) is a voidability provision rather than a private right of action, and only the SEC can enforce the ICA's prohibitions.

splits.relatedScotusTitle: FS Credit Opportunities Corp. v. Saba Capital Master Fund

splits.legalQuestion: Whether Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act, as construed under the Gingles framework, can constitutionally compel race-based redistricting as a remedy for vote dilution, and whether compliance with Section 2 constitutes a compelling governmental interest justifying the use of race in drawing district lines.
splits.description: This case raises the question of whether race-conscious redistricting mandated by Section 2 of the Voting Rights Act is constitutionally permissible under the Equal Protection Clause. Lower courts have applied the Gingles framework to require consideration of race, but the constitutionality of this framework as applied is increasingly contested.

positions.label: Section 2 compels constitutionally permissible race-conscious redistricting
positions.summary: Holds that compliance with Section 2 of the VRA, as interpreted under the Gingles framework, constitutes a compelling interest that justifies the use of race in drawing district lines.

positions.label: Section 2's compelled race-based redistricting raises constitutional concerns
positions.summary: Holds or suggests that the Gingles framework's requirement of race-based redistricting may exceed constitutional limits under the Equal Protection Clause, particularly after Dobbs-era equal protection jurisprudence.

circuits.name: Eighth Circuit
circuits.caseName: Arkansas State Conference of the NAACP v. Arkansas Board of Apportionment
